ending: By the close, El has survived her junior year with her alliance with Aadhya and Liu sealed for the graduation run still a year away, and a warmer but still wary connection to New York's enclave through Chloe Rasmussen - a useful tie, not yet a sealed ally. The year's defining feats are hers: alone, she destroyed a maw-mouth, one of the devouring horrors even enclave wizards can only flee, a kill almost no living wizard can claim, and in the year-end crisis over the graduation hall's failing cleansing machinery she joined the repair effort and unleashed a working that revealed the terrifying scale of the affinity she has spent her life suppressing. Her standing has shifted from feared outcast to someone formidable and worth courting. She has still not touched malia, holding to that line even when it would be easy. She holds the Golden Stone sutras, a working that promises to build an enclave without the usual hidden cost, though she has not yet grasped what that cost is. Her friendship with Orion has become something warmer and harder to name, which makes the final beat land hard: as the new freshmen are inducted, a message from her mother, Gwen, reaches El with a single instruction - stay away from Orion Lake. She is left with no explanation, an alliance to keep alive, a senior year to survive, and a warning she does not understand about the person who has repeatedly saved her life.
in_short: El Higgins is a junior at the Scholomance, a school for young wizards that has no teachers and drifts in the void, where monsters called maleficaria hunt the students and the only way out is a graduation-day run through a hall full of them. Born with an affinity for spells of mass destruction, she refuses the dark magic that would make them easy, and as a poor, unaffiliated girl she has no enclave to shield her. When New York's celebrated Orion Lake keeps saving her life, it costs her the trust of the other students, but she slowly turns classmates into real allies, chiefly the artificer Aadhya and the musician Liu, and settles into an uneasy friendship with Orion. Over the year she uncovers how the enclaves hoard safety, and acquires the Golden Stone sutras, a spell said to build an enclave without its usual hidden cost. She does what almost no wizard can, destroying a maw-mouth alone, and in the year-end crisis helps repair the graduation hall's failing defenses, remaking her reputation from outcast to someone formidable. The book closes as a warning arrives from her mother: keep away from Orion Lake.

recaps.text: El Higgins is grinding through her junior year at the Scholomance, a school with no teachers that hangs in the void and constantly tries to kill its students with maleficaria, monsters that seep in through the vents, drains, and library shelves to feed on young wizards and their magic. El was born with an affinity for large-scale destruction, spells of devastating power that come to her easily, but she refuses to fuel her magic with malia, the corrupting force drawn from other living things, and insists on the slower, cleaner path of mana earned through effort. Poor and unaffiliated, with no enclave to buy her safety, she survives on grit and a sharp tongue. Her mother, Gwen, is a gentle healer in a Welsh commune, which only sets off how prickly and guarded El has become. To her constant irritation, New York's admired Orion Lake keeps rescuing her from mals, and every rescue makes the other students trust her less, since a girl who needs saving looks either weak or, worse, like she is manipulating him. Isolated and defensive, and privately aware that her gift could kill on a horrifying scale if she ever let it, El begins the wary, unglamorous work of turning wary classmates into allies rather than rivals, because no one survives graduation alone.

El's careful alliance-building starts to take real shape. She grows closest to Aadhya, a practical artificer from New Jersey who can build and repair the gear everyone will need, and to Liu, a quiet, musically gifted girl from an independent Chinese wizard family who is distrusted because she keeps mice, which others assume means she dabbles in malia. El, who knows exactly how unfair that kind of suspicion is, chooses to look past it. The daily reality of the school stays brutal: attendance means dodging monsters, guarding your food and water, and weighing every favor as a transaction, since alliances are ultimately negotiations about who will watch whose back when graduation comes. Orion continues to turn up whenever El is in danger, and their mutual annoyance slowly warms toward something like friendship, though El resents needing him and resents more that his heroics keep marking her out. She lets slip, mostly to herself, just how much power she is holding back, and begins to understand that her real problem is not weakness but the constant temptation to solve everything the easy, ruinous way.

As the term wears on, El's loose understandings with Aadhya and Liu firm into a genuine alliance, the first real trust she has allowed herself. The three begin planning in earnest for graduation, pooling skills and thinking about what gear and weapons will carry them through the mal-choked hall that stands between every senior and the outside world. El and Orion drift from open irritation into a real, prickly friendship, and she catches herself relying on him. Meanwhile she keeps studying the shape of the world she is trapped in: how the enclaves, the fortified strongholds of the wealthiest wizard families, hoard mana and safety while independents like her scrape by, and how that inequality follows students right into the school. The ordinary horrors never pause, with monsters massing in the plumbing and the darker corners of the library, and El occasionally lets a glimpse of her true capacity show, enough to unsettle the people around her. Every new bond doubles as a hard bargain about survival, and El, unused to being wanted for anything, navigates it with equal parts suspicion and reluctant hope.

El, Aadhya, and Liu commit to a shared graduation strategy built around a concrete project: after Orion kills an enormous mal called a sirenspider, Aadhya salvages its shell to craft an instrument for Liu, whose music can be shaped into a weapon for the run through the graduation hall. El keeps refusing the shortcut of dark magic even when it would instantly end a deadly problem, and her standing slowly shifts from feared loner toward someone genuinely worth allying with, drawing wary interest even from New York's enclavers. She learns more about how the enclaves are built and sustained, and how much of the wizard world's comfort is quietly bought at the expense of people like her. She also grows uneasy about Orion, whose drive to hunt and destroy monsters is far greater than any ordinary wizard's, an appetite that begins to look less like heroism than like a mystery she cannot name. Around her the school's threat keeps climbing, and El quietly accumulates power, allies, and knowledge, including a book of construction magic that cuts directly against her ruinous affinity and hints that the enclaves' safety need not be built the way it always has been.

As the year's end approaches, the school's dangers come to a head. A student maleficer who had been preying on other students to feed his power is exposed and stopped after turning on El herself, hardening her refusal to ever take that path. El also does what almost no living wizard can claim: alone, she faces a maw-mouth, one of the vast, near-unkillable horrors made of everyone it has swallowed, and destroys it outright, pouring out an enormous store of mana at brutal cost to her reserves and her nerve. When the graduation hall's cleansing machinery proves to be failing, dooming the graduating class above and letting maleficaria leak up into the school, El joins the repair effort, and in the fighting below unleashes a working that helps clear the hall and reveals the terrifying scale of the affinity she has spent her life suppressing. By year's end her standing is remade from pitiable outsider to someone formidable and worth allying with. Her alliance with Aadhya and Liu is sealed for their own graduation run a year away, she has earned a warmer but wary connection to New York through Chloe Rasmussen, and she is closer to Orion than she has let anyone be. That fragile peace breaks with the book's final beat: as the incoming freshmen are inducted, a message reaches her from her mother, Gwen, carrying a single, unexplained warning to keep away from Orion Lake.
